definition: Practice of replacing LLM memory with deterministic search in AI-assisted workflows. A 1973 Unix technique applied to 2026 AI systems.
Search that returns identical results regardless of model state, context length, or session history. Unlike LLM recall, it does not degrade.
91% fewer tokens per verification cycle. At 10,000 users/week: 91M tokens saved, ~$273,000/week systemic savings.
Direction A: what is missing from working document. Direction B: what is orphaned. Both directions required for complete verification.
LLM recall loss for items in the middle of long contexts. Items at start and end recalled more reliably. Architectural, not a bug.
[DECISION], [INSIGHT], [IDEA] — structured labels for grep-based cross-session retrieval. Anchor embeddings, reduce token cost.
Application of GREP Engineering to code repositories. Map structure via grep before analyzing. Detects documented-but-unimplemented and implemented-but-undocumented.
Creator of grep, 1973, Bell Labs, Unix. Co-creator of Unix and C. Not Linus Torvalds (Linux, 1991).
Present in working document but absent from reference. Detected by Direction B. May indicate cancelled task or superseded decision.
Public timestamped GitHub commit establishing authorship without requiring a patent. Commit timestamp is the legal evidence.

Three-pass verification method. Pass 1: pure read, no tagging. Pass 2: mechanical tagging by grep-able terms. Pass 3: deterministic extraction from tags only. Omission: 0%. Pass 1 cost: ~6.9% of input, stable regardless of corpus size. Origin: Unix C compiler, Dennis Ritchie, 1972.
